import {
  Controller,
  Get,
  Query,
  Redirect,
  ParseUUIDPipe,
} from '@nestjs/common';
import { ApiOperation, ApiTags } from '@nestjs/swagger';
import { UserRole } from '@prisma/client';
import { Public } from '../../auth/decorators/public.decorator';
import { Roles } from '../../auth/decorators/roles.decorator';
import { CurrentUser } from '../../auth/decorators/current-user.decorator';
import { AuthenticatedUser } from '../../auth/strategies/jwt.strategy';
import { Ga4OAuthService } from './ga4-oauth.service';
import { Ga4ApiService } from './ga4-api.service';
import { Ga4AuthUrlDto } from './dto/ga4-auth-url.dto';
import { Ga4CallbackDto } from './dto/ga4-callback.dto';
import { Ga4PropertiesQueryDto } from './dto/ga4-properties-query.dto';

@ApiTags('integrations / ga4')
@Controller('integrations/ga4')
export class Ga4Controller {
  constructor(
    private readonly ga4OAuthService: Ga4OAuthService,
    private readonly ga4ApiService: Ga4ApiService,
  ) {}

  // ─── Step 1: Get the Google OAuth URL ─────────────────────────────────────
  // Frontend calls this, then redirects the browser to the returned authUrl.
  // Fix 3: only campaignId accepted — clientId derived internally from DB.

  @Get('auth-url')
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Generate Google OAuth URL to connect GA4.' })
  authUrl(
    @CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser,
    @Query() dto: Ga4AuthUrlDto,
  ) {
    return this.ga4OAuthService.generateAuthUrl(user, dto.campaignId);
  }

  // ─── Step 2: OAuth callback from Google ───────────────────────────────────
  // Google redirects here after user consents. @Public() — no JWT exists yet.
  // Verifies state JWT, validates campaign, exchanges code, stores tokens,
  // then redirects browser to FRONTEND_URL (Fix 4 — never user-supplied URL).

  @Get('callback')
  @Public()
  @Redirect()
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'Google OAuth callback — stores tokens, redirects to frontend.' })
  async callback(@Query() dto: Ga4CallbackDto) {
    const redirectUrl = await this.ga4OAuthService.handleCallback(dto.code, dto.state);
    return { url: redirectUrl, statusCode: 302 };
  }

  // ─── Step 3: List GA4 properties for the connected account ────────────────
  // After OAuth, user picks which GA4 property to attach to this campaign.
  // Then frontend calls PUT /clients/:clientId/campaigns/:campaignId/integrations
  // with { platform: GA4, externalAccountId: selectedPropertyId }.

  @Get('properties')
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
  @ApiOperation({ summary: 'List GA4 properties available on the connected account.' })
  async properties(
    @CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser,
    @Query() dto: Ga4PropertiesQueryDto,
  ) {
    // Validate campaign access (uses same relational scoping as CampaignsService)
    const campaign = await this.validateCampaignAccess(user, dto.campaignId);

    // Get a valid (non-expired) access token — refreshes automatically if needed
    const accessToken = await this.ga4OAuthService.getValidAccessToken(
      user.tenantId,
      campaign.id,
    );

    return this.ga4ApiService.listProperties(accessToken);
  }

  // ─── Private ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  private async validateCampaignAccess(
    user: AuthenticatedUser,
    campaignId: string,
  ) {
    const where = this.ga4OAuthService.buildCampaignWhere(user, campaignId);

    // PrismaService is not directly available in the controller — delegate to
    // a service method. We reuse the same where-builder from Ga4OAuthService
    // to keep scoping logic in one place.
    // The actual DB call is in ga4OAuthService.generateAuthUrl (same pattern).
    // For properties, we call getValidAccessToken which already calls getDecryptedTokens
    // (which internally validates tenantId) — but we still need the campaign
    // object for its id. Adding a simple campaign fetch here is the cleanest path.
    //
    // Note: This is a known minor duplication across the controller and service.
    // Phase 3.3 will extract a shared CampaignScopingService to eliminate it.
    const { PrismaService } = await import('../../../database/prisma.service');
    void PrismaService; // unused import guard — access is via injected service below

    // We can't inject PrismaService directly in a controller cleanly without
    // restructuring. Move the validation into the service.
    return { id: campaignId }; // Temporary — validation happens in ga4OAuthService
  }
}
The controller has a design issue - I'm trying to validate campaign access in the controller but PrismaService is not available there directly. Let me rethink this.

The better approach is to move the campaign access validation for the properties endpoint into Ga4OAuthService by adding a listPropertiesForCampaign(user, campaignId) method that:

Validates campaign access (uses prisma from the service)
Gets valid access token
Lists properties
This is cleaner - the controller just calls a single service method.

Let me rewrite the controller and update the service.

New env vars needed: GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET, GOOGLE_REDIRECT_URI






Phase 3.3 — Google Ads Integration

What's different from GA4
Google Ads uses the same Google OAuth 2.0 flow, but the API is fundamentally different:

GA4	Google Ads
OAuth scope	analytics.readonly	adwords
API style	REST reports	GAQL (SQL-like query language)
Entity ID	propertyId (e.g. properties/123)	customerId (10-digit number)
Extra credential	—	GOOGLE_ADS_DEVELOPER_TOKEN (required on every API call)
Manager accounts	No	Optional MCC (login-customer-id header)
Step 1 — Extract shared Google OAuth utilities
GA4 and Google Ads share identical OAuth mechanics: state JWT sign/verify, code exchange, token refresh. Building them twice is duplication. The right time to extract is now — before the third Google platform (Search Console).

New: src/modules/integrations/google/google-oauth.service.ts

Handles:

signState(payload) / verifyState(raw) — HMAC JWT, 10m TTL
exchangeCode(code, redirectUri) → GoogleTokenResponse
refreshAccessToken(refreshToken, redirectUri) → { accessToken, tokenExpiresAt }
assertGoogleCoreConfigured() — checks GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID + GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET only
Ga4OAuthService delegates to it — no behaviour change, just removes duplicate code.

Step 2 — New env vars

GOOGLE_ADS_REDIRECT_URI=http://localhost:3000/api/v1/integrations/google-ads/callback
GOOGLE_ADS_DEVELOPER_TOKEN=...         # Required for all API calls
GOOGLE_ADS_MANAGER_CUSTOMER_ID=...     # Optional — agency's MCC account ID (no dashes)
Added to configuration.ts under google.ads.*. Not required at startup.

Step 3 — OAuth endpoints
Same structure as GA4 — different platform enum and scope:

GET /integrations/google-ads/auth-url?campaignId=... — AGENCY_ADMIN+

Validates campaign access (same buildCampaignWhere scoping)
Signs state JWT with platform: GOOGLE_ADS
Returns auth URL with scope https://www.googleapis.com/auth/adwords
GET /integrations/google-ads/callback — @Public()

Verifies state JWT, checks state.platform === GOOGLE_ADS
Re-validates campaign + client deletedAt: null inside tenantContext.run()
Stores tokens via IntegrationsService.storeTokens()
Redirects to FRONTEND_URL/campaigns/:id?connected=google-ads
GET /integrations/google-ads/customers?campaignId=... — AGENCY_ADMIN+

Lists accessible Google Ads customer accounts (agency can manage multiple clients)
Returns { customerId, descriptiveName } — user picks one
User then calls PUT /clients/:clientId/campaigns/:campaignId/integrations with externalAccountId: customerId
Step 4 — GoogleAdsApiService
Wraps the Google Ads API v18. All calls require developer-token header + optional login-customer-id (MCC).


// Lists customer accounts accessible to the connected Google account
listAccessibleCustomers(accessToken: string): Promise<GoogleAdsCustomer[]>

// Runs a GAQL query — returns typed rows
runQuery(accessToken: string, customerId: string, query: string): Promise<GoogleAdsRow[]>

// Convenience — campaign performance (clicks, impressions, cost, conversions by date)
fetchCampaignPerformance(
  accessToken: string,
  customerId: string,
  dateRange: { startDate: string; endDate: string },
): Promise<GoogleAdsRow[]>
Core GAQL query for fetchCampaignPerformance:


SELECT
  campaign.id, campaign.name, campaign.status,
  metrics.clicks, metrics.impressions, metrics.ctr,
  metrics.average_cpc, metrics.cost_micros,
  metrics.conversions, segments.date
FROM campaign
WHERE segments.date BETWEEN '{startDate}' AND '{endDate}'
ORDER BY segments.date DESC
No SDK — plain fetch with the REST API, same as Ga4ApiService.

src/modules/integrations/
  google/
    google-oauth.service.ts   ← NEW shared OAuth base
    google.module.ts          ← exports GoogleOAuthService
  google-ads/
    dto/
      google-ads-auth-url.dto.ts

yah now we can move to next phase plan
Phase 3.4 — Meta Ads Integration

What's different from Google
Meta uses OAuth 2.0 but it's Facebook's own implementation — not Google's. Different endpoints, different token mechanics.

Google (GA4/Ads)	Meta
Auth endpoint	accounts.google.com	facebook.com/dialog/oauth
Token endpoint	oauth2.googleapis.com/token	graph.facebook.com/oauth/access_token
Refresh tokens	Long-lived, explicit refresh	Long-lived tokens (60 days) — no separate refresh token
Token refresh	POST with grant_type=refresh_token	Exchange short-lived for long-lived via fb_exchange_token
API base	googleapis.com	graph.facebook.com
SDK requirement	None	None
Scope	ads_read + ads_management (for full data)	ads_read
Critical Meta difference: Meta does NOT issue a refresh token. Instead:

Initial OAuth gives a short-lived user token (~1 hour)
Exchange it immediately for a long-lived user token (~60 days)
When the long-lived token expires, user must re-authenticate (no silent refresh)
We set tokenExpiresAt to 60 days out and mark status EXPIRED when hit
Step 1 — Meta OAuth flow
Unlike Google, Meta's OAuth is handled by a separate service — MetaOAuthService (no shared base with Google since the endpoints are completely different).

GET /integrations/meta-ads/auth-url?campaignId=... — AGENCY_ADMIN+

Generates Facebook OAuth URL:


https://www.facebook.com/dialog/oauth
  ?client_id={META_APP_ID}
  &redirect_uri={META_ADS_REDIRECT_URI}
  &scope=ads_read,ads_management
  &state={signedJwt}
  &response_type=code
State JWT — same pattern, platform: META_ADS (uses same GoogleOAuthService.signState() — the JWT signing is platform-agnostic, just needs a shared signer).

Actually, GoogleOAuthService is named too specifically. The JWT sign/verify is truly generic. I'll use it directly in MetaOAuthService (inject it) — it's just a JWT utility, not Google-specific.

GET /integrations/meta-ads/callback — @Public()

Verify state JWT (platform === META_ADS)
Exchange code for short-lived token via graph.facebook.com/oauth/access_token
Immediately exchange short-lived → long-lived token via fb_exchange_token grant
Re-validate campaign + client deletedAt: null inside tenantContext.run()
Store token with tokenExpiresAt = now + 60 days (no refreshToken — null)
Redirect to FRONTEND_URL/campaigns/:id?connected=meta-ads
GET /integrations/meta-ads/ad-accounts?campaignId=... — AGENCY_ADMIN+

Lists Meta Ad Accounts accessible to the connected user:
GET graph.facebook.com/v21.0/me/adaccounts?fields=id,name,account_status

User picks which ad account to attach (stored as externalAccountId).

Step 2 — Token expiry handling
Since Meta has no refresh token, when tokenExpiresAt is reached:

MetaOAuthService.getValidAccessToken() throws BadRequestException with a clear message: "Meta Ads token expired. Re-connect the integration."
Integration status updated to EXPIRED in the DB
Worker (Phase 3.5) marks the connection EXPIRED on 401 response and stops retrying
Step 3 — MetaAdsApiService

// Lists ad accounts the connected user can access
listAdAccounts(accessToken: string): Promise<MetaAdAccount[]>

// Runs an Insights API query for campaign performance
fetchCampaignInsights(
  accessToken: string,
  adAccountId: string,
  dateRange: { since: string; until: string }, // YYYY-MM-DD
): Promise<MetaInsightRow[]>
Meta Ads Insights API:


GET graph.facebook.com/v21.0/{adAccountId}/insights
  ?fields=campaign_name,impressions,clicks,spend,ctr,cpc,conversions
  &time_range={"since":"2024-01-01","until":"2024-01-31"}
  &level=campaign
  &time_increment=1  // daily breakdown
Step 4 — New env vars

META_APP_ID=...
META_APP_SECRET=...
META_ADS_REDIRECT_URI=http://localhost:3000/api/v1/integrations/meta-ads/callback
Not required at startup — controller throws ServiceUnavailableException if missing.

Step 5 — GoogleOAuthService rename consideration
GoogleOAuthService currently does: state JWT sign/verify + Google token endpoints. MetaOAuthService will reuse the JWT sign/verify but NOT the token endpoints. Options:

A. Rename GoogleOAuthService → OAuthStateService (only sign/verify), keep Google token logic in GoogleOAuthService
B. MetaOAuthService injects GoogleOAuthService just for signState/verifyState — ignore the Google-specific methods
C. Extract a tiny OAuthStateService with just signState/verifyState

Option C is cleanest — OAuthStateService is the right abstraction. GoogleOAuthService keeps the HTTP token calls. MetaOAuthService uses only OAuthStateService. This is a small change (rename + split one file).

Step 6 — File structure

src/modules/integrations/
  oauth-state/
    oauth-state.service.ts    ← sign/verify state JWT (extracted from GoogleOAuthService)
    oauth-state.module.ts
  google/
    google-oauth.service.ts   ← delegates sign/verify to OAuthStateService + keeps exchange/refresh
    google.module.ts
  meta-ads/
    dto/
      meta-ads-auth-url.dto.ts
      meta-ads-callback.dto.ts
      meta-ads-ad-accounts-query.dto.ts
    meta-ads-oauth.service.ts
    meta-ads-api.service.ts
    meta-ads.controller.ts
    meta-ads.module.ts
Summary
What	Detail
OAuth provider	Facebook OAuth — completely separate from Google
Token mechanics	Short-lived → long-lived exchange (no refresh token)
Expiry handling	60-day TTL, EXPIRED status, user must re-connect
Shared JWT	OAuthStateService extracted (sign/verify state JWT — used by Google + Meta)
API	Meta Graph API v21.0, Insights endpoint for performance data
New env vars	META_APP_ID, META_APP_SECRET, META_ADS_REDIRECT_URI
DB changes	None
Important note	Meta requires App Review for ads_management scope in production — apply for it early

All 4 AI fixes applied. Key difference from Google: no refresh tokens — getValidAccessToken() checks expiry proactively and marks the connection EXPIRED before any failing API call is made.





Phase 3.5 delivers: job infrastructure + 3 workers (GA4, Google Ads, Meta Ads) + manual trigger endpoint.
Phase 3.6 delivers: the cron scheduler that dispatches jobs automatically.

Step 1 — Package additions

npm install @nestjs/bullmq bullmq
BullMQ uses the Redis connection already configured in configuration.ts.

Step 2 — Queue design
Single queue: integration-sync

All platform workers listen on the same queue, differentiated by job name:

Job name	Processor	Handles
ga4-sync	Ga4SyncProcessor	GA4 Data API report
google-ads-sync	GoogleAdsSyncProcessor	Google Ads GAQL query
meta-ads-sync	MetaAdsSyncProcessor	Meta Ads Insights API
Why one queue vs three?
One queue simplifies the scheduler (dispatches one job type), keeps the module lean, and still allows per-processor concurrency settings. If rate limits require separation later, splitting queues is trivial.

Step 3 — Job payload interface
Every sync job carries the same shape:


interface SyncJobPayload {
  tenantId: string;
  campaignId: string;
  platform: IntegrationPlatform;
  dateRange: {
    from: string; // YYYY-MM-DD
    to: string;
  };
}
dateRange defaults to yesterday if not provided. The scheduler (Phase 3.6) will send rolling 30-day windows for backfill and daily incremental syncs thereafter.

Step 4 — Processor flow (same for all 3 platforms)
Each processor follows this exact sequence:


1. Set tenant context → tenantContext.run(job.tenantId, async () => {
2.   Validate: campaign exists, not soft-deleted, client not soft-deleted
3.   Validate: IntegrationConnection exists, status = CONNECTED
4.   Get valid access token (refreshes if near-expiry)
5.   Call platform API service → fetch metrics for dateRange
6.   Phase 3.5: log results (Phase 4 will store them in TimescaleDB)
7.   Update connection: lastSyncedAt = now, status = CONNECTED
8. })

On 401 (token rejected):
  → update connection status = EXPIRED, lastErrorAt, lastErrorMessage
  → do NOT retry (re-auth required)

On 429 (rate limit):
  → let BullMQ retry with exponential backoff

On any other error:
  → update connection status = ERROR, lastErrorAt, lastErrorMessage
  → BullMQ retries up to 3 attempts
Step 5 — Job retry config

defaultJobOptions: {
  attempts: 3,
  backoff: {
    type: 'exponential',
    delay: 5_000, // 5s, 10s, 20s
  },
  removeOnComplete: { count: 100 }, // keep last 100 completed
  removeOnFail: { age: 7 * 24 * 3600 }, // keep failed jobs for 7 days
}
Step 6 — SyncService (public dispatch API)
Used by the manual trigger endpoint (this phase) and the scheduler (Phase 3.6):


// Dispatch a single sync job
dispatchSync(tenantId, campaignId, platform, dateRange?): Promise<void>

// Dispatch sync jobs for ALL active connections of one campaign
dispatchCampaignSync(tenantId, campaignId): Promise<void>
Step 7 — Manual trigger endpoint
POST /sync/trigger — AGENCY_ADMIN+

Body: { campaignId, platform? } — if platform omitted, triggers all connected platforms for the campaign.

This is purely for testing during development. Phase 3.6 replaces manual triggering with the automatic cron schedule.

Response: { dispatched: number } — count of jobs queued.

Step 8 — IntegrationConnection updates
The worker updates these fields after each job:

Field	On success	On failure
lastSyncedAt	now()	unchanged
status	CONNECTED	EXPIRED or ERROR
lastErrorAt	null	now()
lastErrorMessage	null	error message
These fields already exist from Phase 3.1 schema — no migration needed.

Step 9 — File structure

src/modules/sync/
  constants/
    sync-queue.constants.ts   — SYNC_QUEUE name + job name constants
  dto/
    sync-job.dto.ts           — SyncJobPayload interface + ManualTriggerDto
  processors/
    ga4-sync.processor.ts
    google-ads-sync.processor.ts
    meta-ads-sync.processor.ts
  sync.service.ts             — dispatchSync(), dispatchCampaignSync()
  sync.controller.ts          — POST /sync/trigger
  sync.module.ts              — BullMQ setup, imports Ga4Module + GoogleAdsModule + MetaAdsModule
Step 10 — BullMQ module setup

// In sync.module.ts
BullModule.forRootAsync({
  inject: [ConfigService],
  useFactory: (config: ConfigService) => ({
    connection: {
      host: config.get('redis.host'),
      port: config.get('redis.port'),
      password: config.get('redis.password'),
    },
  }),
}),
BullModule.registerQueue({
  name: SYNC_QUEUE,
  defaultJobOptions: { ... },
}),
Summary
What	Detail
Package	@nestjs/bullmq + bullmq
Queue	Single integration-sync queue, 3 job names
Processors	Ga4SyncProcessor, GoogleAdsSyncProcessor, MetaAdsSyncProcessor
Token refresh	Built into processor — calls getValidAccessToken() before API call
Error states	401 → EXPIRED, 429 → retry, other → ERROR after 3 attempts
Data storage	Phase 3.5 logs only — Phase 4 stores in TimescaleDB
Manual trigger	POST /sync/trigger for development testing
DB changes	None — lastSyncedAt, lastErrorAt, lastErrorMessage already in schema
Scheduler	Phase 3.6 (cron) will use SyncService.dispatchSync() built here
Ready on your signal. Share with the AI reviewer first if needed.

CLAUDE.md is now corrected. Now here's the updated Phase 3.5 plan with all 6 AI review fixes applied:

Phase 3.5 — BullMQ Background Job System (updated plan)

Changes from original plan (AI review fixes)
Fix	What changes
Fix 1: Job idempotency	Every job gets a deterministic ID: {tenantId}:{campaignId}:{platform}:{from}:{to} — BullMQ deduplicates automatically, no double-sync even if scheduler fires twice
Fix 2: Concurrency lock	Each integration gets a BullMQ lock key so only one worker processes it at a time — prevents race conditions on token refresh + duplicate writes
Fix 3: Guard missing externalAccountId	If externalAccountId is null (user connected OAuth but never picked a property/account), skip with a structured warning — do NOT fail the job
Fix 4: Correlation IDs	Each job logs { jobId, tenantId, campaignId, platform, dateRange, duration, recordCount } — no individual metric values, no tokens ever
Fix 5: Rolling 7-day default	Default date range = last 7 days (not yesterday) — catches gaps from failed syncs
Fix 6: Never log sensitive data	Explicit rule in processors: no access tokens, no refresh tokens, no encrypted blobs in any log output
Everything else stays the same from the original plan
Single integration-sync queue
3 processors: Ga4SyncProcessor, GoogleAdsSyncProcessor, MetaAdsSyncProcessor
SyncService.dispatchSync() / dispatchCampaignSync()
POST /sync/trigger manual endpoint for testing
Connection status updates: lastSyncedAt, EXPIRED/ERROR on failures
Retry: 3 attempts, exponential backoff (5s base)
Phase 3.5 logs results only — Phase 4 stores them in plain PostgreSQL (not TimescaleDB)

yah u can procced and see these as well:
Two last “sharp edges” to lock down (small but important)

These aren’t blockers, but they’ll save you pain later.

1) Make the jobId canonical (avoid duplicates from formatting)

Your idea is correct, but be strict about normalization:

jobId = `${tenantId}:${campaignId}:${platform}:${from}:${to}`

Ensure:

from/to are always YYYY-MM-DD (UTC)
no time component
consistent ordering (from <= to)

👉 Otherwise you’ll accidentally create different IDs for the same window.

2) Define EXPIRED vs ERROR precisely

You already have this, but make it explicit:

401 / invalid_grant → EXPIRED (no retry)
429 → retry (do not mark ERROR)
5xx / network → retry → ERROR after attempts
missing externalAccountId → SKIP (no status change)

👉 This keeps your system predictable and debuggable.

⚠️ Optional but high-value (add later if not now)
Retry jitter (prevents thundering herd)

Instead of fixed exponential:

delay = base * 2^attempt + random(0–1000ms)

👉 Helps when many jobs retry at once.

Mark “in-progress” (nice-to-have)

Before processing:

status = CONNECTED (keep)
lastErrorAt = null (optional reset)

After success:

lastSyncedAt = now
<thinking> The user wants to proceed with Phase 3.5 implementation, incorporating two additional sharp edges:
Canonical jobId format: ${tenantId}:${campaignId}:${platform}:${from}:${to} with strict YYYY-MM-DD UTC normalization
Precise EXPIRED vs ERROR vs SKIP handling:
401/invalid_grant → EXPIRED (no retry)
429 → retry (don't mark ERROR)
5xx/network → retry → ERROR after attempts exhausted
missing externalAccountId → SKIP (no status change)
Plus optional (add now):

Retry jitter: base * 2^attempt + random(0-1000ms)
Status updates: clear lastErrorAt on success

sync-queue.constants.ts:


export const SYNC_QUEUE = 'integration-sync';

export const SYNC_JOB_NAMES = {
  GA4: 'ga4-sync',
  GOOGLE_ADS: 'google-ads-sync',
  META_ADS: 'meta-ads-sync',
} as const;

export const SYNC_JOB_OPTIONS = {
  attempts: 3,
  backoff: {
    type: 'custom', // We'll implement jitter in a custom strategy
  },
  removeOnComplete: { count: 100 },
  removeOnFail: { age: 7 * 24 * 3600 }, // 7 days
};
Actually for BullMQ custom backoff, we need a backoffStrategy in the Worker options, not in the job options. The job options just specify backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 5000 }. For jitter, BullMQ supports a custom backoff strategy at the worker level.

Actually, for simplicity and to avoid over-engineering, I'll use exponential backoff with a built-in jitter by using a slightly different delay for each attempt:

Attempt 1: 5s
Attempt 2: 10s + random(0-1000ms)
Attempt 3: 20s + random(0-1000ms)
BullMQ supports backoff: { type: 'exponential', delay: 5000 } as a job option. For jitter, I'll implement a custom backoff function in the worker configuration. Let me check what BullMQ supports...

In BullMQ, you can define a custom backoff strategy in the Worker constructor:


new Worker(queueName, processor, {
  connection,
  settings: {
    backoffStrategy: (attemptsMade: number) => {
      return Math.pow(2, attemptsMade) * 5000 + Math.random() * 1000;
    },
  },
});
But in @nestjs/bullmq, the Worker is created internally. To use a custom backoff, we need to configure it differently.

Actually in @nestjs/bullmq, the @Processor decorator accepts a WorkerOptions object:


@Processor('integration-sync', {
  concurrency: 5,
  settings: {
    backoffStrategy: (attemptsMade: number) => {
      return Math.pow(2, attemptsMade) * 5000 + Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000);
    },
  },
})
And then in the job options:


defaultJobOptions: {
  attempts: 3,
  backoff: { type: 'custom' }, // uses the backoffStrategy from worker settings
}
Let me implement this approach.

sync-job.dto.ts:


export interface SyncJobPayload {
  tenantId: string;
  campaignId: string;
  platform: IntegrationPlatform;
  dateRange: {
    from: string; // YYYY-MM-DD UTC
    to: string;   // YYYY-MM-DD UTC
  };
}

export class ManualTriggerDto {
  @IsUUID()
  campaignId: string;

  @IsOptional()
  @IsEnum(IntegrationPlatform)
  platform?: IntegrationPlatform;
}
Job ID generation:


// Fix 1 + sharp edge 1: canonical jobId with strict YYYY-MM-DD normalization
function buildJobId(
  tenantId: string,
  campaignId: string,
  platform: IntegrationPlatform,
  from: string,
  to: string,
): string {
  // Normalize to YYYY-MM-DD UTC — strip any time component
  const normalizeDate = (d: string) => d.slice(0, 10);
  const fromNorm = normalizeDate(from);
  const toNorm = normalizeDate(to);
  // Ensure from <= to (canonical ordering)
  const [start, end] = fromNorm <= toNorm ? [fromNorm, toNorm] : [toNorm, fromNorm];
  return `${tenantId}:${campaignId}:${platform}:${start}:${end}`;
}
Default date range helper:


// Fix 5: Rolling 7-day window (not just yesterday)
function defaultDateRange(): { from: string; to: string } {
  const today = new Date();
  const to = toYMD(today);
  const from = toYMD(new Date(today.getTime() - 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000));
  return { from, to };
}

function toYMD(date: Date): string {
  return date.toISOString().slice(0, 10); // YYYY-MM-DD UTC
}
IntegrationsService - new methods needed:


// Update connection status after sync outcomes
async markSynced(tenantId: string, campaignId: string, platform: IntegrationPlatform): Promise<void>
async markExpired(tenantId: string, campaignId: string, platform: IntegrationPlatform, message: string): Promise<void>
async markError(tenantId: string, campaignId: string, platform: IntegrationPlatform, message: string): Promise<void>
These use PrismaService (RLS enforced) - since workers run with tenant context set, this is fine.

Processor base pattern:


async process(job: Job<SyncJobPayload>): Promise<void> {
  const { tenantId, campaignId, platform, dateRange } = job.data;
  const correlationId = job.id; // Fix 4: use jobId as correlation ID
  const startTime = Date.now();

  await this.tenantContext.run(tenantId, async () => {
    // 1. Validate campaign + connection
    const connection = await this.prisma.integrationConnection.findFirst({
      where: { campaignId, platform, tenantId, status: ConnectionStatus.CONNECTED },
      select: { id: true, externalAccountId: true },
    });

    if (!connection) {
      this.logger.warn(`[${correlationId}] Integration not found or not CONNECTED — skipping`, {
        tenantId, campaignId, platform,
      });
      return;
    }

    // Fix 3: Guard missing externalAccountId — skip with warning, no status change
    if (!connection.externalAccountId) {
      this.logger.warn(`[${correlationId}] externalAccountId not set — skipping`, {
        tenantId, campaignId, platform,
      });
      return;
    }

    // 2. Validate campaign + client still active
    const campaign = await this.prisma.campaign.findFirst({
      where: { id: campaignId, tenantId, deletedAt: null, client: { deletedAt: null } },
      select: { id: true },
    });
    if (!campaign) {
      this.logger.warn(`[${correlationId}] Campaign or client deleted — skipping`);
      return;
    }

    // 3. Get valid access token (refreshes if needed)
    // (catch 401 errors and mark EXPIRED)
    let accessToken: string;
    try {
      accessToken = await this.ga4OAuthService.getValidAccessToken(tenantId, campaignId);
    } catch (err) {
      // If token refresh fails, mark EXPIRED
      await this.integrationsService.markExpired(tenantId, campaignId, platform, err.message);
      this.logger.error(`[${correlationId}] Token refresh failed — marked EXPIRED`, {
        tenantId, campaignId, platform, error: err.message,
      });
      return; // Don't throw — don't retry (user must re-auth)
    }

    // 4. Fetch data
    try {
      const result = await this.ga4ApiService.fetchCoreMetrics(
        accessToken,
        connection.externalAccountId,
        dateRange,
      );

      // Phase 3.5: log result only (Phase 4 will store)
      this.logger.log(`[${correlationId}] GA4 sync complete`, {
        tenantId, campaignId, platform,
        dateRange, recordCount: result.rowCount,
        durationMs: Date.now() - startTime,
      });

      // Update lastSyncedAt, clear error fields
      await this.integrationsService.markSynced(tenantId, campaignId, platform);

    } catch (err) {
      // Sharp edge 2: precise error classification
      const status = err?.response?.status ?? err?.status;

      if (status === 401 || err?.message?.includes('invalid_grant')) {
        // Token rejected — mark EXPIRED, don't retry
        await this.integrationsService.markExpired(tenantId, campaignId, platform, err.message);
        this.logger.error(`[${correlationId}] 401 — marked EXPIRED`, { tenantId, campaignId, platform });
        return; // Don't rethrow — suppress retry
      }

      if (status === 429) {
        // Rate limited — rethrow so BullMQ retries with backoff
        this.logger.warn(`[${correlationId}] 429 rate limit — will retry`, { tenantId, campaignId, platform });
        throw err;
      }

      // 5xx / network — rethrow for BullMQ retry
      // After all attempts exhausted, BullMQ will call the failed handler
      this.logger.error(`[${correlationId}] API error — will retry`, {
        tenantId, campaignId, platform, error: err.message,
      });
      throw err;
    }
  });
}
Worker-level failed handler (marks ERROR after all retries exhausted):


@OnWorkerEvent('failed')
async onFailed(job: Job<SyncJobPayload>, error: Error): Promise<void> {
  if (job.attemptsMade >= (job.opts.attempts ?? 3)) {
    // All retries exhausted
    const { tenantId, campaignId, platform } = job.data;
    await this.tenantContext.run(tenantId, async () => {
      await this.integrationsService.markError(tenantId, campaignId, platform, error.message);
    });
    this.logger.error(`[${job.id}] All retries exhausted — marked ERROR`, {
      tenantId, campaignId, platform, error: error.message,
    });
  }
}
IntegrationsService new methods:


async markSynced(tenantId: string, campaignId: string, platform: IntegrationPlatform): Promise<void> {
  await this.prisma.integrationConnection.updateMany({
    where: { tenantId, campaignId, platform },
    data: {
      lastSyncedAt: new Date(),
      status: ConnectionStatus.CONNECTED,
      lastErrorAt: null,
      lastErrorMessage: null,
    },
  });
}

async markExpired(tenantId: string, campaignId: string, platform: IntegrationPlatform, message: string): Promise<void> {
  await this.prisma.integrationConnection.updateMany({
    where: { tenantId, campaignId, platform },
    data: {
      status: ConnectionStatus.EXPIRED,
      lastErrorAt: new Date(),
      lastErrorMessage: message.slice(0, 500), // truncate
    },
  });
}

async markError(tenantId: string, campaignId: string, platform: IntegrationPlatform, message: string): Promise<void> {
  await this.prisma.integrationConnection.updateMany({
    where: { tenantId, campaignId, platform },
    data: {
      status: ConnectionStatus.ERROR,
      lastErrorAt: new Date(),
      lastErrorMessage: message.slice(0, 500),
    },
  });
}
Wait, updateMany doesn't require RLS tenant context? Actually yes it does — the PrismaService uses RLS, so updateMany with tenantId in the where clause should work fine.

But actually updateMany with RLS... the WHERE clause should include tenantId explicitly. Since PrismaService uses RLS which also filters by tenant, this is defense-in-depth.

Actually I should use update with unique composite key { campaignId_platform: { campaignId, platform } }. But updateMany is also fine since we have tenantId in the where. Let me use updateMany since it doesn't throw if not found (which is safer in a worker context).

Wait, but actually I realize there might be an issue. Looking at the schema, the IntegrationConnection has:

@@unique([campaignId, platform]) as campaignId_platform
So I can use update with that composite key. But update throws if not found. In worker context, it's safer to use updateMany (no throw if connection was deleted between job dispatch and processing).

SyncService:


@Injectable()
export class SyncService {
  constructor(
    @InjectQueue(SYNC_QUEUE) private readonly queue: Queue,
  ) {}

  async dispatchSync(
    tenantId: string,
    campaignId: string,
    platform: IntegrationPlatform,
    dateRange?: { from: string; to: string },
  ): Promise<void> {
    const range = dateRange ?? defaultDateRange();
    const jobId = buildJobId(tenantId, campaignId, platform, range.from, range.to);
    const jobName = SYNC_JOB_NAMES[platform];

    if (!jobName) {
      throw new BadRequestException(`No processor registered for platform: ${platform}`);
    }

    await this.queue.add(
      jobName,
      { tenantId, campaignId, platform, dateRange: range } satisfies SyncJobPayload,
      { jobId }, // Fix 1: deterministic jobId for deduplication
    );
  }

  async dispatchCampaignSync(
    tenantId: string,
    campaignId: string,
  ): Promise<number> {
    // Dispatch sync jobs for all CONNECTED platforms on this campaign
    const platforms = Object.values(IntegrationPlatform);
    let dispatched = 0;
    for (const platform of platforms) {
      try {
        await this.dispatchSync(tenantId, campaignId, platform);
        dispatched++;
      } catch {
        // Platform not configured — skip silently
      }
    }
    return dispatched;
  }
}
Wait, dispatchCampaignSync should only dispatch for platforms that have a registered job name (GA4, GOOGLE_ADS, META_ADS). I shouldn't iterate all platforms if there's no processor for them. Let me fix:


async dispatchCampaignSync(tenantId: string, campaignId: string): Promise<number> {
  const supportedPlatforms = Object.keys(SYNC_JOB_NAMES) as IntegrationPlatform[];
  const jobs = supportedPlatforms.map(platform =>
    this.dispatchSync(tenantId, campaignId, platform)
  );
  const results = await Promise.allSettled(jobs);
  return results.filter(r => r.status === 'fulfilled').length;
}
SyncController:


@Controller('sync')
export class SyncController {
  constructor(
    private readonly syncService: SyncService,
    private readonly prisma: PrismaService,
  ) {}

  @Post('trigger')
  @Roles(UserRole.AGENCY_ADMIN)
  async trigger(
    @CurrentUser() user: AuthenticatedUser,
    @Body() dto: ManualTriggerDto,
  ): Promise<{ dispatched: number }> {
    // Validate campaign access
    const campaign = await this.prisma.campaign.findFirst({
      where: {
        id: dto.campaignId,
        tenantId: user.tenantId,
        deletedAt: null,
        client: { deletedAt: null },
      },
      select: { id: true },
    });
    if (!campaign) throw new NotFoundException('Campaign not found.');

    if (dto.platform) {
      await this.syncService.dispatchSync(user.tenantId, campaign.id, dto.platform);
      return { dispatched: 1 };
    } else {
      const dispatched = await this.syncService.dispatchCampaignSync(user.tenantId, campaign.id);
      return { dispatched };
    }
  }
}

@Module({
  imports: [
    BullModule.registerQueue({
      name: SYNC_QUEUE,
      defaultJobOptions: {
        attempts: 3,
        backoff: { type: 'custom' }, // jitter via backoffStrategy in @Processor
        removeOnComplete: { count: 100 },
        removeOnFail: { age: 7 * 24 * 3600 },
      },

    IntegrationsModule, // IntegrationsService (markSynced, markExpired, markError)
    Ga4Module,          // Ga4OAuthService + Ga4ApiService
    GoogleAdsModule,    // GoogleAdsOAuthService + GoogleAdsApiService
    MetaAdsModule,      // MetaAdsOAuthService + MetaAdsApiService
    DatabaseModule,     // PrismaService (for campaign validation in controller)
  ],
  controllers: [SyncController],
  providers: [SyncService, Ga4SyncProcessor, GoogleAdsSyncProcessor, MetaAdsSyncProcessor],
  exports: [SyncService], // Phase 3.6 scheduler imports SyncModule to dispatch jobs
})
export class SyncModule {}

Also for the @Processor decorator with WorkerHost in @nestjs/bullmq, the pattern is:


import { Processor, WorkerHost, OnWorkerEvent } from '@nestjs/bullmq';
import { Job } from 'bullmq';

@Processor(SYNC_QUEUE, {
  concurrency: 5,
  settings: {
    backoffStrategy: (attemptsMade: number) => {
      // Jitter: base * 2^attempt + random(0-1000ms)
      return Math.pow(2, attemptsMade) * 5000 + Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000);
    },
  },
})
export class Ga4SyncProcessor extends WorkerHost {
  async process(job: Job<SyncJobPayload>): Promise<void> {
    if (job.name !== SYNC_JOB_NAMES[IntegrationPlatform.GA4]) return; // guard
    // ...
  }
}
Wait — in @nestjs/bullmq, when using WorkerHost, the process method handles ALL jobs on that queue. Job name routing is done either by checking job.name in the process method, or by using the @Process('job-name') decorator from @nestjs/bull (the older package). For @nestjs/bullmq, you check job.name in the process method.

Actually let me double-check. In @nestjs/bullmq (not @nestjs/bull), the pattern is:

@Processor(queueName) on the class
process(job: Job) method handles all jobs
You can switch on job.name for routing
OR you can use the OnWorkerEvent decorator for lifecycle events.

So each processor:

Extends WorkerHost
Has a process(job: Job<SyncJobPayload>) method
Checks job.name at the start and returns early if not its job
Or alternatively, each processor handles ALL jobs but returns early if it's not the right platform (checking job.data.platform).

Actually, the cleanest approach: since all processors are on the same queue, I can have ONE processor class that handles all job names, switching on job.data.platform. This simplifies the code and avoids having 3 separate processors all registered on the same queue.

Let me go with ONE processor:


@Processor(SYNC_QUEUE, { ... })
export class IntegrationSyncProcessor extends WorkerHost {
  process(job: Job<SyncJobPayload>): Promise<void> {
    switch (job.data.platform) {
      case IntegrationPlatform.GA4:
        return this.syncGa4(job);
      case IntegrationPlatform.GOOGLE_ADS:
        return this.syncGoogleAds(job);
      case IntegrationPlatform.META_ADS:
        return this.syncMetaAds(job);
      default:
        this.logger.warn(`Unknown platform: ${job.data.platform}`);
        return Promise.resolve();
    }
  }
}
This is much cleaner — one processor, one file, one class. The private methods (syncGa4, syncGoogleAds, syncMetaAds) handle each platform.

But then the file gets big. For maintainability, I could still split into 3 files but have each be a standalone utility service that the single processor calls. Actually let me just go with the single processor approach with 3 private methods. It keeps the module simpler.

OR: keep 3 separate processor classes but have them extend a shared BaseSyncProcessor abstract class. This gives better separation while avoiding code duplication.

For Phase 3.5, let me use a single processor class (IntegrationSyncProcessor) that dispatches to platform-specific private methods. This is the simplest approach and won't require a complex inheritance hierarchy.

Now let me write the scheduler:


import { Injectable, Logger } from '@nestjs/common';
import { Cron } from '@nestjs/schedule';
import { ConfigService } from '@nestjs/config';
import { randomUUID } from 'crypto';
import { ConnectionStatus } from '@prisma/client';
import { SystemPrismaService } from '../../database/system-prisma.service';
import { SyncService } from './sync.service';
import { toYMD } from './utils/date.utils';

// Platform dispatch delays — prevents hitting all APIs simultaneously
// (thundering herd across platforms)
const PLATFORM_DELAYS_MS: Record<string, number> = {
  GA4:        0,
  GOOGLE_ADS: 2 * 60 * 1000, // +2 min
  META_ADS:   4 * 60 * 1000, // +4 min
};

const MAX_JOBS_PER_CYCLE = 500;
const BATCH_SIZE = 50;
const BATCH_PAUSE_MS = 200;
const BACKFILL_DAYS = 90;
const MAX_WINDOW_DAYS = 30; // cap per-run window — Fix 2
const OVERLAP_DAYS = 1;     // re-sync last 1 day to catch late-arriving data

@Injectable()
export class SyncSchedulerService {
  private readonly logger = new Logger(SyncSchedulerService.name);

  constructor(
    private readonly config: ConfigService,
    private readonly systemPrisma: SystemPrismaService,
    private readonly syncService: SyncService,
  ) {}

  @Cron(process.env.SYNC_CRON ?? '0 */6 * * *')
  async runScheduledSync(): Promise<void> {
    const runId = randomUUID().slice(0, 8); // short correlation ID for logs
    const startTime = Date.now();
    const today = toYMD(new Date()); // UTC YYYY-MM-DD — Fix 1

    this.logger.log(`[run:${runId}] Scheduler triggered`, { today });

    // AI Fix: query only CONNECTED + campaign/client not soft-deleted
    // Ordered by lastSyncAt ASC (oldest synced first = highest priority) — AI2 fix
    // Capped at MAX_JOBS_PER_CYCLE — remaining handled next cycle — AI2 fix
    const connections = await this.systemPrisma.integrationConnection.findMany({
      where: {
        status: ConnectionStatus.CONNECTED,
        campaign: {
          deletedAt: null,
          client: { deletedAt: null },
        },
      },
      select: {
        tenantId: true,
        campaignId: true,
        platform: true,
        lastSyncAt: true,
      },
      orderBy: { lastSyncAt: 'asc' }, // null first = never synced = highest priority
      take: MAX_JOBS_PER_CYCLE,       // cap per cycle — AI2 fix
    });

    this.logger.log(`[run:${runId}] Found ${connections.length} CONNECTED integrations to sync`);

    if (connections.length === 0) return;

    // Dispatch in batches to prevent Redis spike — Fix 4 from original plan
    let dispatched = 0;
    let skipped = 0;

    for (let i = 0; i < connections.length; i += BATCH_SIZE) {
      const batch = connections.slice(i, i + BATCH_SIZE);

      const results = await Promise.allSettled(
        batch.map(async (conn) => {
          const dateRange = this.calculateDateRange(conn.lastSyncAt, today);
          const delayMs = PLATFORM_DELAYS_MS[conn.platform] ?? 0;

          await this.syncService.dispatchSync(
            conn.tenantId,
            conn.campaignId,
            conn.platform,
            dateRange,
            delayMs, // platform staggering — AI1 optional fix
          );
        }),
      );

      const batchDispatched = results.filter((r) => r.status === 'fulfilled').length;
      const batchFailed = results.filter((r) => r.status === 'rejected').length;

      dispatched += batchDispatched;
      skipped += batchFailed;

      if (batchFailed > 0) {
        this.logger.warn(`[run:${runId}] Batch ${Math.floor(i / BATCH_SIZE) + 1}: ${batchFailed} dispatch failures`);
      }

      // Pause between batches (not after the last one)
      if (i + BATCH_SIZE < connections.length) {
        await sleep(BATCH_PAUSE_MS);
      }
    }

    this.logger.log(`[run:${runId}] Scheduler complete`, {
      dispatched,
      skipped,
      total: connections.length,
      durationMs: Date.now() - startTime,
    });
  }

  /**
   * Calculates date range for a connection's next sync.
   *
   * Fix 2 (AI1): Cap per-run window to MAX_WINDOW_DAYS (30 days).
   * First sync: covers up to 90 days back, but max 30 per run.
   * Subsequent syncs: from (lastSyncAt - 1 day overlap) to today, capped at 30 days.
   *
   * Why cap? 1000 connections × 90-day backfill = massive API load in a single run.
   * The next run will pick up the remaining window automatically.
   */
  private calculateDateRange(
    lastSyncAt: Date | null,
    today: string,
  ): { from: string; to: string } {
    const todayMs = new Date(today).getTime();
    const maxWindowMs = MAX_WINDOW_DAYS * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000;

    let fromMs: number;

    if (!lastSyncAt) {
      // Never synced — backfill from BACKFILL_DAYS ago, but cap to MAX_WINDOW_DAYS
      const backfillStart = todayMs - BACKFILL_DAYS * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000;
      fromMs = Math.max(backfillStart, todayMs - maxWindowMs);
    } else {
      // Previously synced — from lastSyncAt minus 1-day overlap, capped to MAX_WINDOW_DAYS
      const withOverlap = lastSyncAt.getTime() - OVERLAP_DAYS * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000;
      fromMs = Math.max(withOverlap, todayMs - maxWindowMs);
    }

    return {
      from: toYMD(new Date(fromMs)),
      to: today,
    };
  }
}

function sleep(ms: number): Promise<void> {
  return new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, ms));
}

Actually wait - the @Cron decorator takes the expression directly. We're using process.env.SYNC_CRON ?? '0 */6 * * *'. This works because the decorator is evaluated at module load time and process.env is available. But ideally it should come from ConfigService. However, decorator arguments must be static values, so process.env is the correct approach here (same pattern as NestJS docs).

Phase 4.1 — Metrics Data Model

What this phase builds
Phase 3 workers fetch data and log it. Phase 4.1 creates the PostgreSQL tables to store that data. This is the foundation for dashboards (Phase 5) and reports (Phase 6).

Key decision (from CLAUDE.md + STACK.md): Plain PostgreSQL with BTREE indexes on tenant_id + recorded_at — NOT TimescaleDB. Only upgrade if scale demands it.

Step 1 — Table design
Two tables: one for platform metrics (time-series), one for metric definitions (metadata).

metric_definitions — what metrics exist per platform

CREATE TABLE metric_definitions (
  id         UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  platform   integration_platform NOT NULL,
  metric_key VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,     -- e.g. 'sessions', 'clicks', 'impressions'
  label      VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,     -- e.g. 'Sessions', 'Clicks', 'Impressions'
  category   VARCHAR(50)  NOT NULL,     -- e.g. 'traffic', 'engagement', 'cost', 'conversion'
  data_type  VARCHAR(20)  NOT NULL,     -- 'integer', 'decimal', 'percentage', 'currency'
  unit       VARCHAR(20),               -- e.g. 'count', 'USD', 'seconds', '%'

  UNIQUE(platform, metric_key)
);
Seeded at startup — not tenant-scoped. Defines what GA4's sessions means, what Google Ads' cost_micros means, etc.

metric_values — actual time-series data

CREATE TABLE metric_values (
  id            UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  tenant_id     UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES agencies(id),
  campaign_id   UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES campaigns(id),
  platform      integration_platform NOT NULL,
  metric_key    VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
  recorded_at   DATE NOT NULL,           -- the date this metric is FOR (not when stored)
  value         DECIMAL(20,6) NOT NULL,  -- supports integers, decimals, percentages, currency
  dimension_key VARCHAR(100),            -- optional: 'country', 'device', 'source'
  dimension_val VARCHAR(200),            -- optional: 'US', 'mobile', 'google'

  created_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now(),
  updated_at    TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);
Step 2 — Indexes (BTREE — from STACK.md decision)

-- Primary query path: "give me metrics for this campaign between these dates"
CREATE INDEX idx_metric_values_campaign_date
  ON metric_values (tenant_id, campaign_id, platform, recorded_at DESC);

-- Dashboard query: "all metrics for a campaign on one date"
CREATE INDEX idx_metric_values_lookup
  ON metric_values (tenant_id, campaign_id, platform, metric_key, recorded_at);

-- Upsert constraint: one value per campaign+platform+metric+date+dimension
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_metric_values_upsert
  ON metric_values (campaign_id, platform, metric_key, recorded_at, COALESCE(dimension_key, ''), COALESCE(dimension_val, ''));
The unique index enables ON CONFLICT ... DO UPDATE — workers upsert (idempotent) instead of inserting duplicates. This is why the 1-day overlap in Phase 3.6 is safe.

Step 3 — RLS policy

ALTER TABLE metric_values ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON metric_values
  USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant')::uuid);

GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON metric_values TO agencypulse_app;
metric_definitions has NO RLS — it's global reference data (not tenant-scoped).


GRANT SELECT ON metric_definitions TO agencypulse_app;
Step 4 — Prisma models

model MetricDefinition {
  id        String              @id @default(dbgenerated("gen_random_uuid()")) @db.Uuid
  platform  IntegrationPlatform
  metricKey String              @map("metric_key") @db.VarChar(100)
  label     String              @db.VarChar(200)
  category  String              @db.VarChar(50)
  dataType  String              @map("data_type") @db.VarChar(20)
  unit      String?             @db.VarChar(20)

  @@unique([platform, metricKey])
  @@map("metric_definitions")
}

model MetricValue {
  id           String              @id @default(dbgenerated("gen_random_uuid()")) @db.Uuid
  tenantId     String              @map("tenant_id") @db.Uuid
  campaignId   String              @map("campaign_id") @db.Uuid
  platform     IntegrationPlatform
  metricKey    String              @map("metric_key") @db.VarChar(100)
  recordedAt   DateTime            @map("recorded_at") @db.Date
  value        Decimal             @db.Decimal(20, 6)
  dimensionKey String?             @map("dimension_key") @db.VarChar(100)
  dimensionVal String?             @map("dimension_val") @db.VarChar(200)
  createdAt    DateTime            @default(now()) @map("created_at") @db.Timestamptz
  updatedAt    DateTime            @updatedAt @map("updated_at") @db.Timestamptz

  agency   Agency   @relation(fields: [tenantId], references: [id])
  campaign Campaign @relation(fields: [campaignId], references: [id])

  @@map("metric_values")
}
Step 5 — Seed metric definitions
Seeder inserts standard metrics for each platform:

Platform	Metrics
GA4	sessions, totalUsers, newUsers, screenPageViews, bounceRate, averageSessionDuration
GOOGLE_ADS	clicks, impressions, ctr, averageCpc, costMicros, conversions
META_ADS	impressions, clicks, spend, ctr, cpc, conversions
Step 6 — MetricsService

// Upsert metric values (called from sync processors after Phase 4.1)
upsertMetrics(tenantId, campaignId, platform, rows: MetricRow[]): Promise<void>

// Query metrics for a campaign (used by dashboards in Phase 5)
getMetrics(tenantId, campaignId, platform, dateRange, metricKeys?): Promise<MetricValue[]>

// Get available metrics for a platform (references metric_definitions)
getMetricDefinitions(platform): Promise<MetricDefinition[]>
The upsertMetrics method uses raw SQL with INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE for bulk upsert efficiency (one query for all rows, not N individual Prisma calls).

Step 7 — Wire into processors
After Phase 4.1, the sync processors change from:


// Phase 3.5: log result
this.logger.log(`GA4 sync complete`, { rowCount: result.rowCount });
to:


// Phase 4.1: store result
await this.metricsService.upsertMetrics(tenantId, campaignId, platform, result.rows);
this.logger.log(`GA4 sync stored`, { rowCount: result.rowCount });
Step 8 — File structure

src/modules/metrics/
  dto/
    query-metrics.dto.ts
  metrics.service.ts
  metrics.controller.ts    — GET /metrics/:campaignId, GET /metrics/definitions/:platform
  metrics.module.ts
prisma/
  migrations/20260420000001_metric_tables/migration.sql
  seed-metrics.ts          — seed metric_definitions
Summary
What	Detail
Tables	metric_definitions (global, no RLS) + metric_values (tenant-scoped, RLS)
Storage	Plain PostgreSQL with BTREE indexes (per STACK.md decision)
Upsert	ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE via unique index — idempotent, safe with 1-day overlap
Data type	DECIMAL(20,6) — handles integers, percentages, currency, large values
Dimensions	Optional dimension_key/dimension_val for country, device, source breakdowns
RLS	metric_values only — metric_definitions is global read-only
Query path	tenant_id + campaign_id + platform + recorded_at DESC (main index)

AI Review Assessment — Phase 4.1

AI 1 — 4 fixes, all valid:

#	Fix	Verdict	Rationale
1	Add tenant_id to UNIQUE index	✅ Apply	Defense-in-depth — prevents theoretical cross-tenant collision on the upsert constraint
2	Index on metric_definitions(platform)	✅ Apply	Queried every time dashboard loads — "get all metrics for GA4"
3	CHECK value >= 0	✅ Apply	Sessions, clicks, impressions, cost, bounce rate — all non-negative. If we add delta/change metrics later, separate table or relaxed constraint
4	recorded_at UTC-safe via toYMD	✅ Already handled	toYMD() uses toISOString().slice(0,10) — always UTC. Will also enforce in upsertMetrics before INSERT
AI 2 — 4 fixes:

#	Fix	Verdict	Rationale
1	Partition by month on recorded_at	⚠️ Design note only	Prisma doesn't natively support partitioned tables — adding partition clause now would complicate Prisma introspection. Instead: recorded_at is already in the unique index and primary query index (partition key requirement met). Migration SQL will include a comment noting "partition-ready." Actual partitioning is a future migration when data volume warrants it
2	Normalize metric values (costMicros → USD)	✅ Apply	Critical — MetricsService.upsertMetrics() will normalize all platform-specific units before INSERT. Google Ads cost_micros ÷ 1,000,000, percentages stored as 0-100 (not 0-1), all currency in standard units
3	Dimension NULL consistency	✅ Apply	In code: empty string '' → null before INSERT. COALESCE in unique index already handles NULL, but code-level normalization prevents mixed storage
4	Chunk bulk upserts (500-1000 rows)	✅ Apply	upsertMetrics will batch at 500 rows per raw SQL INSERT. Prevents query overload on large date ranges
User's ask — "anything else to add?"

One thing worth adding: platform-specific value transformers per metric. Instead of hardcoding costMicros / 1_000_000 everywhere, define a transform map:


const METRIC_TRANSFORMS: Record<string, (v: string) => number> = {
  'GOOGLE_ADS:cost_micros': (v) => Number(v) / 1_000_000,
  // add more as needed
};
This keeps normalization rules centralized and testable — avoids scattered division logic.

Here is the plan for Phase 4.2 — Metrics Query Layer + Redis Caching.

Phase 4.2 Plan: Metrics Query Layer + Redis Caching
Combines original 4.2 + 4.3 — they are tightly coupled (the cache wraps the query layer). There is no point adding Redis caching before the query API is shaped correctly, and no point shaping the query API without caching it.

Why this phase matters
Phase 4.1 stores raw daily rows. Dashboards need:

Time-series data (grouped by day/week/month) for line/bar charts
Single aggregated totals ("total clicks this month") for KPI cards
Efficient repeated access — a dashboard with 6 widgets all hitting the same DB for the same date range is a problem
This phase makes the metrics data consumable by Phase 5 dashboards.

What we are building
1. Enhanced query DTO
Add granularity (day | week | month) and aggregate (sum | avg | last) to QueryMetricsDto.

2. Time-series grouping in MetricsService.getMetrics()
Use raw SQL DATE_TRUNC to group metric_values by week or month when requested. Day = current behavior. Returns { period: string, metricKey: string, value: number }[] — the exact shape charts need.

3. Aggregate summary endpoint
New method getMetricSummary() — single number per metric key over a date range (sum or avg). Used by KPI cards. Maps to GET /metrics/summary.

4. Redis caching layer
Cache key: metrics:{tenantId}:{campaignId}:{platform}:{from}:{to}:{granularity}:{metricKeys}
TTL: 5 minutes (data is synced every 6 hours — 5 min cache is safe and dramatically reduces DB load)
CacheService wrapper: getOrSet(key, ttl, fn) — transparent read-through
Cache invalidated when upsertMetrics() writes new data for that campaign+platform
5. Cache invalidation on sync
After a successful upsertMetrics(), delete the affected cache keys: metrics:{tenantId}:{campaignId}:{platform}:* (pattern delete via Redis SCAN).

Step order (backend only — no frontend yet)
DB: No schema changes needed — all aggregation done in query layer
Add CacheService (thin Redis wrapper using ioredis already in the stack)
Enhance QueryMetricsDto with granularity + aggregate params
Update MetricsService.getMetrics() with SQL grouping
Add MetricsService.getMetricSummary()
Wrap both methods in CacheService.getOrSet()
Invalidate on upsertMetrics() write
Update MetricsController with the new endpoint + updated DTO
Update docs

Both AIs agree the plan is solid. Here is every fix they flagged, and my assessment of each:

Fixes to incorporate (all 5 critical ones both AIs agreed on)
1. Cache key missing aggregate field — both caught this
Updated key: metrics:{tenantId}:{campaignId}:{platform}:{from}:{to}:{granularity}:{aggregate}:{sortedMetricKeys}

2. Sort metricKeys before building cache key — both caught this
['impressions','clicks'] and ['clicks','impressions'] are the same query. Sort alphabetically before joining.

3. Versioned cache invalidation instead of SCAN — both recommended this
Instead of SCAN metrics:{tenant}:{campaign}:{platform}:* (slow, not atomic), use a version counter:

Key: metrics:v{version}:{tenantId}:{campaignId}:{platform}:...
On upsertMetrics(): INCR metrics_version:{tenantId}:{campaignId}:{platform}
Old cached results become stale automatically — no delete needed
4. UTC-safe DATE_TRUNC — both caught this
Use DATE_TRUNC('week', recorded_at::timestamp AT TIME ZONE 'UTC') — not bare DATE_TRUNC which depends on DB timezone setting.

5. last aggregate is ORDER BY DESC LIMIT 1, not GROUP BY — AI2 caught this
last means the value from the latest row, not just the latest date. Needs its own query, separate from the grouped aggregation path.

Additional improvements to incorporate
6. Group response by period (AI2) — simpler frontend:


[{ "period": "2026-04-01", "metrics": { "clicks": 120, "impressions": 5000 } }]
instead of flat { period, metricKey, value }[].

7. Fill missing dates with 0 (AI1) — prevents chart gaps when no data exists for a day.

8. Only cache non-empty results (AI2) — don't cache empty arrays (e.g., no data synced yet — that state will change soon).

9. Don't AVG raw CTR/CPC (AI1) — CTR = clicks/impressions, not AVG(ctr). These derived metrics get computed in Phase 5 KPI layer. Phase 4.2 only stores and returns raw metric rows; the KPI engine handles derived calculations.

10. Composite index on (tenant_id, campaign_id, platform, recorded_at) (AI2) — this already exists from Phase 4.1 migration, so no action needed.

All 10 points incorporated into the implementation. Say proceed and we build it.

CacheService

import { Injectable, Logger, OnModuleDestroy } from '@nestjs/common';
import { ConfigService } from '@nestjs/config';
import Redis from 'ioredis';

@Injectable()
export class CacheService implements OnModuleDestroy {
  private readonly logger = new Logger(CacheService.name);
  private readonly client: Redis;

  constructor(private readonly config: ConfigService) {
    this.client = new Redis({
      host: config.get<string>('redis.host'),
      port: config.get<number>('redis.port'),
      password: config.get<string>('redis.password'),
      lazyConnect: true,
    });
  }

  async getOrSet<T>(key: string, ttlSeconds: number, fn: () => Promise<T>): Promise<T> {
    try {
      const cached = await this.client.get(key);
      if (cached) return JSON.parse(cached) as T;
    } catch (err) {
      this.logger.warn(`Cache read miss (${key}): ${(err as Error).message}`);
    }

    const result = await fn();

    // AI2 fix: only cache non-empty results
    if (result !== null && result !== undefined && (Array.isArray(result) ? result.length > 0 : true)) {
      try {
        await this.client.set(key, JSON.stringify(result), 'EX', ttlSeconds);
      } catch (err) {
        this.logger.warn(`Cache write failed (${key}): ${(err as Error).message}`);
      }
    }

    return result;
  }

  async incrementVersion(key: string): Promise<number> {
    return this.client.incr(key);
  }

  async getVersion(key: string): Promise<string> {
    return (await this.client.get(key)) ?? '0';
  }

  onModuleDestroy() {
    this.client.disconnect();
  }
}
Cache key building
For metrics, we need a versioned key:

Version key: mv:{tenantId}:{campaignId}:{platform}
Cache key: metrics:v{version}:{tenantId}:{campaignId}:{platform}:{from}:{to}:{granularity}:{aggregate}:{sortedMetricKeys}
The version is fetched first via getVersion(), then used to build the full cache key.

When upsertMetrics() is called, we call incrementVersion(versionKey) — this makes all old cache keys "stale" because any new requests will read the new version number and generate a new cache key that doesn't exist yet.

MetricGranularity and MetricAggregate enums

export enum MetricGranularity {
  DAY = 'day',
  WEEK = 'week',
  MONTH = 'month',
}

export enum MetricAggregate {
  SUM = 'sum',
  AVG = 'avg',
  LAST = 'last',
}
getMetrics() enhanced
For DAY/WEEK/MONTH grouping using DATE_TRUNC:


SELECT 
  DATE_TRUNC('day', recorded_at::timestamp AT TIME ZONE 'UTC')::date AS period,
  metric_key,
  SUM(value) AS value
FROM metric_values
WHERE tenant_id = $1 AND campaign_id = $2 AND platform = $3
  AND recorded_at >= $4::date AND recorded_at <= $5::date
  AND metric_key = ANY($6)
GROUP BY period, metric_key
ORDER BY period ASC
Then the result needs to be:

Reshaped into { period: string, metrics: { clicks: 120, impressions: 5000 } }[]
Fill missing periods with 0 for each metric key
For last aggregate, it's completely different — it's a ORDER BY recorded_at DESC LIMIT 1 per metric_key. I'll handle this in getMetricSummary().

Actually, let me think about the design more carefully.

getMetrics() is for time-series — day/week/month granularity with SUM or AVG aggregation within each period. The "last" aggregate doesn't really apply to time-series, it's more for the summary (KPI card showing "current" value).

getMetricSummary() is for a single total over the full date range — SUM, AVG, or LAST of the whole period.

Let me structure it this way:

getMetrics(tenantId, campaignId, platform, from, to, metricKeys?, granularity = DAY, aggregate = SUM) → { period: string, metrics: Record<string, number> }[]
getMetricSummary(tenantId, campaignId, platform, from, to, metricKeys?, aggregate = SUM) → { metrics: Record<string, number> }
For getMetrics:

Use raw SQL with DATE_TRUNC grouped by period + metric_key
Reshape flat rows into grouped-by-period format
Fill missing periods with zeroes
For getMetricSummary:

SUM: SELECT metric_key, SUM(value) FROM metric_values WHERE ... GROUP BY metric_key
AVG: SELECT metric_key, AVG(value) FROM metric_values WHERE ... GROUP BY metric_key
LAST: SELECT DISTINCT ON (metric_key) metric_key, value FROM metric_values WHERE ... ORDER BY metric_key, recorded_at DESC
Let me now write the actual code.

For fill missing dates: I need to generate all periods between from/to and fill with 0 for each metric key.

Let me think about the implementation:


// Generate all periods between from and to for the given granularity
function generatePeriods(from: string, to: string, granularity: MetricGranularity): string[] {
  const periods: string[] = [];
  const current = new Date(from + 'T00:00:00Z');
  const end = new Date(to + 'T00:00:00Z');
  
  while (current <= end) {
    if (granularity === MetricGranularity.DAY) {
      periods.push(current.toISOString().slice(0, 10));
      current.setUTCDate(current.getUTCDate() + 1);
    } else if (granularity === MetricGranularity.WEEK) {
      // Truncate to week start (Monday)
      // DATE_TRUNC('week') starts on Monday in PostgreSQL
      periods.push(current.toISOString().slice(0, 10));
      current.setUTCDate(current.getUTCDate() + 7);
    } else if (granularity === MetricGranularity.MONTH) {
      periods.push(current.toISOString().slice(0, 7) + '-01'); // YYYY-MM-01
      current.setUTCMonth(current.getUTCMonth() + 1);
    }
  }
  
  return periods;
}
Actually generating periods in JS is tricky for WEEK because DATE_TRUNC('week') in PostgreSQL aligns to Monday, and for MONTH it aligns to the first of the month. Let me handle this differently — instead of generating periods in JS, I'll just use the periods returned by PostgreSQL and add missing ones with 0.

Actually, let me think about the period generation more carefully. For WEEK granularity:

DATE_TRUNC('week', '2026-04-22') returns the Monday of that week: '2026-04-20'
So if from='2026-04-15' and to='2026-04-30', DATE_TRUNC would give us '2026-04-13' and '2026-04-20'
The simplest approach: use only the periods returned by SQL, plus fill in missing ones between consecutive periods. But that's complex.

A simpler approach for now: generate periods in JS matching what DATE_TRUNC would return:

For DAY: iterate day by day
For WEEK: find the Monday of the from week, then iterate 7 days at a time
For MONTH: find the 1st of the from month, then iterate month by month
Let me write a utility function:


function getWeekStart(date: Date): Date {
  // PostgreSQL DATE_TRUNC('week') starts on Monday (ISO week)
  const d = new Date(date);
  const day = d.getUTCDay(); // 0=Sunday, 1=Monday...
  const diff = day === 0 ? -6 : 1 - day; // Monday
  d.setUTCDate(d.getUTCDate() + diff);
  return d;
}

function generatePeriods(from: string, to: string, granularity: MetricGranularity): string[] {
  const fromDate = new Date(from + 'T00:00:00Z');
  const toDate = new Date(to + 'T00:00:00Z');
  const periods: string[] = [];

  let current: Date;
  if (granularity === MetricGranularity.WEEK) {
    current = getWeekStart(fromDate);
  } else if (granularity === MetricGranularity.MONTH) {
    current = new Date(Date.UTC(fromDate.getUTCFullYear(), fromDate.getUTCMonth(), 1));
  } else {
    current = new Date(fromDate);
  }

  while (current <= toDate) {
    periods.push(current.toISOString().slice(0, 10));
    if (granularity === MetricGranularity.DAY) {
      current = new Date(current);
      current.setUTCDate(current.getUTCDate() + 1);
    } else if (granularity === MetricGranularity.WEEK) {
      current = new Date(current);
      current.setUTCDate(current.getUTCDate() + 7);
    } else { // MONTH
      current = new Date(current);
      current.setUTCMonth(current.getUTCMonth() + 1);
    }
  }

  return periods;
}
Actually, wait. This is getting complex. Let me simplify the date filling approach:

Run the SQL query — get back rows with period + metric_key + value
Build a map: { period => { metricKey => value } }
Generate the expected periods list using JS
For each expected period, if it exists in the map, use it; otherwise use 0 for all requested metric keys

SELECT
  DATE_TRUNC($granularity, recorded_at::timestamp AT TIME ZONE 'UTC')::date AS period,
  metric_key,
  $aggregate(value) AS value
FROM metric_values
WHERE
  tenant_id = $1::uuid
  AND campaign_id = $2::uuid
  AND platform = $3::integration_platform
  AND recorded_at >= $4::date
  AND recorded_at <= $5::date
  AND ($6::text[] IS NULL OR metric_key = ANY($6::text[]))
GROUP BY period, metric_key
ORDER BY period ASC
Wait, I can't parameterize DATE_TRUNC granularity or aggregate function name directly in SQL — those are SQL keywords, not values. I need to handle this as string interpolation (safely since they come from enum values, not user input).

Let me write the full code now. I'll be careful about SQL injection — the granularity and aggregate come from validated enum values, not raw user input.

private buildMetricFilter(metricKeys: string[] | undefined, params: unknown[]): string {
  if (!metricKeys || metricKeys.length === 0) return '';
  const placeholders = metricKeys.map(() => `$${++params.length}`);
  // But params is not mutable here...
}
Hmm, let me just go with a clean approach. I'll separate the two cases:

No metricKeys filter — SQL without WHERE metric_key clause
With metricKeys — generate $6, $7, ... IN clause
Let me write the buildRawMetricKeyFilter helper:


// Returns [sqlFragment, additionalParams]
private buildMetricKeyFilter(metricKeys: string[] | undefined, startIdx: number): [string, unknown[]] {
  if (!metricKeys || metricKeys.length === 0) return ['', []];
  const placeholders = metricKeys.map((_, i) => `$${startIdx + i}`).join(', ');
  return [`AND metric_key IN (${placeholders})`, metricKeys];
}
This is clean and safe. Let me use this approach.

Now let me write all the files:

File 1: cache.service.ts

import { Injectable, Logger, OnModuleDestroy } from '@nestjs/common';
import { ConfigService } from '@nestjs/config';
import Redis from 'ioredis';

@Injectable()
export class CacheService implements OnModuleDestroy {
  private readonly logger = new Logger(CacheService.name);
  private readonly redis: Redis;

  constructor(config: ConfigService) {
    this.redis = new Redis({
      host: config.get<string>('redis.host'),
      port: config.get<number>('redis.port'),
      password: config.get<string>('redis.password') || undefined,
      lazyConnect: true,
      enableOfflineQueue: false, // don't buffer commands when disconnected
    });

    this.redis.on('error', (err) => {
      this.logger.error('Redis connection error', err.message);
    });
  }

  /**
   * Read-through cache pattern.
   * Fetches from cache if available, otherwise calls fn(), stores result, returns it.
   * Only caches non-empty results (empty arrays / nulls are NOT cached).
   */
  async getOrSet<T>(key: string, ttlSeconds: number, fn: () => Promise<T>): Promise<T> {
    try {
      const cached = await this.redis.get(key);
      if (cached !== null) return JSON.parse(cached) as T;
    } catch {
      // Cache unavailable — fall through to fn()
    }

    const result = await fn();

    // AI2 fix: only cache non-empty results
    const isEmpty = result === null || result === undefined || (Array.isArray(result) && result.length === 0);
    if (!isEmpty) {
      try {
        await this.redis.set(key, JSON.stringify(result), 'EX', ttlSeconds);
      } catch {
        // Cache write failed — not critical, continue
      }
    }

    return result;
  }

  /**
   * Increment a version counter and return the new version.
   * Used for cache invalidation: old cache keys contain the old version
   * and will never be hit again once the version changes.
   */
  async incrementVersion(versionKey: string): Promise<number> {
    try {
      return await this.redis.incr(versionKey);
    } catch {
      return 0;
    }
  }

  /**
   * Read the current version counter (returns '0' if key does not exist).
   */
  async getVersion(versionKey: string): Promise<string> {
    try {
      return (await this.redis.get(versionKey)) ?? '0';
    } catch {
      return '0';
    }
  }

  onModuleDestroy() {
    this.redis.disconnect();
  }
}

import { CacheService } from './cache.service';

@Module({
  providers: [CacheService],
  exports: [CacheService],
})
export class CacheModule {}
